resources.string: Demo city guide with Drawer, Bottom Navigation and Navigation Component.
Use the bottom tabs for top-level screens or open the drawer to switch categories.
A short list of places that work well for a first city walk.
This sample project demonstrates a host screen with DrawerLayout, bottom navigation, category destinations and a detail screen built on the Jetpack Navigation component.
The settings screen is intentionally simple. It can be expanded with theme changes, text scale or route filters in future iterations.
Warm places for studying and meetings
Places with local history and art
A calm coffee bar with large windows, soft music and sockets near most tables. Good for a short meeting or an evening study session.
Specialty coffee near the historical block
A convenient stop after classes when you want coffee and a short walk through the old quarter.
Reliable for breakfast meetings, laptop work and a quiet recharge in the middle of the day.
A more silent coffee spot for reading and writing when you want fewer distractions.
Useful if you want coffee before a riverside walk or while guiding a visitor through the center.
A classic option for a calm walk. The long route works well for conversation, photos and a quiet evening.
Short green break close to the busy streets
Benches, trees and a simple walking loop make this a good reset point between errands.
A more secluded route for slow walks, fewer cars and more shade in warm months.
Open area for a longer walk
A spacious route with room for jogging, meeting friends and changing pace after indoor work.
A practical stop when you need a short rest, quick orientation and a few city photos.
Start here for context about the city
A good first stop when you want the rest of the route to feel more meaningful.
Adds variety to the guide and gives a contemporary contrast to classical museum stops.
This recommendation mixes architecture and exhibitions, which many guests enjoy.
Interactive room for students and families
A lighter museum stop when you want something more hands-on in the middle of the route.
A niche recommendation that makes the city guide feel more personal and less generic.
Good for snacks, practical purchases and a more everyday side of the city.
Convenient when you need several basic shops in one place and do not want to change districts.
Useful when visitors want to take home something local, light and easy to pack.
A practical option with cafés, stationery and daily essentials for a compact schedule.
An easy finish for the route if the group wants to eat, rest and solve small errands in one stop.
